questions.question: Which of the following is equivalent to $\log_{b}(x) + \log_{b}(y)$?
questions.explanation: The Product Property states that the sum of two logarithms with the same base is equal to the logarithm of their product.

questions.question: Expand the expression $\ln(x^3)$.
questions.explanation: Using the Power Property, the exponent 3 within the argument is moved to the front of the logarithm as a coefficient.

questions.question: Condense the expression $2 \log(x) - \log(y)$.
questions.explanation: Apply the Power Property to make the coefficient 2 an exponent ($x^2$), then use the Quotient Property to change the subtraction into division.

questions.question: Is $\log(x - y)$ equal to $\log(x) - \log(y)$?
options: Yes, this is the Quotient Property.
No, there is no property for the log of a difference.
questions.explanation: Log properties exist for products, quotients, and powers. There is no property that allows you to expand the logarithm of a subtraction or addition.

questions.question: Expand $\log_{2}( rac{5}{x})$.
questions.explanation: The Quotient Property converts the division of two values inside a logarithm into the subtraction of the individual logarithms.

questions.question: Evaluate $\log_{b}(b)$.
questions.explanation: Since $b^1 = b$, the exponent required to reach the base from itself is always 1.

questions.question: Which property allows us to write $\ln\sqrt{x}$ as $ rac{1}{2} \ln(x)$?
questions.explanation: Because $\sqrt{x}$ can be written as $x^{1/2}$, the Power Property allows the exponent $1/2$ to move to the front.

questions.question: Simplify $\log_{3}(27x)$.
questions.explanation: First, split the product: $\log_{3}(27) + \log_{3}(x)$. Since $3^3 = 27$, the numerical part simplifies to 3.

questions.question: True or False: $\log(x) + \log(y) = \log(x + y)$?
questions.explanation: This is false. The sum of logs is the log of the product ($xy$), not the log of the sum ($x+y$).

questions.question: What is the value of $\ln(e^x)$?
questions.explanation: By the Power Property, $x \ln(e)$. Since $\ln(e) = 1$, the expression simplifies to $x$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've got the laws of logs down pat.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Be careful not to invent your own properties for addition/subtraction!
scoreMessages.50: Review the three main properties—they are the key to the rest of the unit.
scoreMessages.0: Focus on the Product, Quotient, and Power properties.
